**June 13 update:** Board vote on $181M budget shortfall remains scheduled for June 18 — 5 DAYS. No delay announced despite earlier speculation. 558 positions affected districtwide: 215 teaching positions, 228 vacant roles, and 115 other staff. District has identified 73% of the $132M in required spending cuts so far ($85M central office + $25M campus cuts). Boundary realignment workshops June 16 (3 DAYS) and June 22-23. Teachers union urging district to use remaining time before the vote to minimize impacts to currently employed staff. Staff, students, and parents voiced concerns at June 9 public session. CDBG public comment opens June 17 (4 DAYS). Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 14 update:** Board vote on $181M budget shortfall remains scheduled for June 18 — 4 DAYS — but board members discussed DELAYING TO JUNE 25 at the June 11 information session. Several trustees questioned how they could vote just days after receiving revised numbers that showed the general fund deficit swelling to $95M, with projected reserves falling below board policy minimums. 558 positions affected districtwide. Boundary realignment workshops June 16 (2 DAYS) and June 22-23. CDBG public comment opens June 17 (3 DAYS). Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth. Raising Travis County virtual town hall June 16 at 6 PM (2 DAYS).

**June 15 update:** Board vote on $181M budget shortfall remains on the June 18 agenda — 3 DAYS — no formal postponement announced, though board members discussed delaying to June 25 at the June 11 session. General fund deficit at $95M; 558 positions affected. Boundary realignment community workshops TOMORROW (June 16) and June 22-23 — these will shape the school consolidation map. CDBG public comment opens June 17 (2 DAYS). Raising Travis County virtual town hall TOMORROW (June 16) at 6 PM. Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 16 update:** Board vote on $181M budget shortfall remains on the June 18 agenda — 2 DAYS — no formal postponement announced. General fund deficit at $95M; 558 positions affected. Boundary realignment community workshops TODAY (June 16) and June 22-23. CDBG public comment opens TOMORROW (June 17) through July 20, public hearing July 14. Raising Travis County virtual town hall TODAY at 6 PM. Travis County offices closed June 18-19 for Juneteenth. Impacted staff will receive official notices June 19 if the vote proceeds as scheduled.

**June 17 update:** Board vote on $181M budget shortfall is TOMORROW (June 18). Written public comments accepted through June 18 at 5 PM at www.austinisd.org. 558 positions affected including teachers, librarians, and campus police. Key strategies: $60M from selling/monetizing four AISD properties, $31M from increasing student-to-teacher ratios and cutting librarians, $17M from central office cuts, $31M through attrition. Budget must pass by end of June. CDBG public comment period opens TODAY (June 17) through July 20. Travis County offices CLOSED June 18-19 for Juneteenth.

**June 18 update:** ⚡ BOARD VOTE TODAY — Trustees voting on $181M budget shortfall for 2026-27. Written public comments accepted through 5 PM at www.austinisd.org. 558 positions eliminated including teachers, librarians, counselors, campus police, and part-time assistant principals. Key strategies: $60M from selling/monetizing four AISD properties, $31M from larger class sizes and cutting librarians, $17M from central office cuts, $31M through attrition. 10 campus closures already approved in November save ~$21M. Budget must pass by end of June. Travis County offices CLOSED today and tomorrow for Juneteenth — 37th Annual celebration at 800 Lavaca 11 AM-2 PM. CDBG public comment open through July 20.

**June 19 update:** ⚡ AISD BUDGET APPROVED 7-1 — Board passed $205M in cuts last night (up from $181M originally planned). Last-minute amendment restored full-time librarians at all campuses (~$1M investment from the district's fund balance). One trustee abstained. 558 positions eliminated including teachers, counselors, campus police, part-time assistant principals. Key strategies: $60M from selling/monetizing four AISD properties, $31M from larger class sizes, $17M from central office cuts, $31M through attrition, ~$21M from 10 campus closures approved in November. Budget must be filed with TEA by end of June. Juneteenth — AISD offices CLOSED today.

public_comment.instructions: AISD board meetings allow public comment. Next: May 9 virtual budget meeting, 11am-noon. May 21 recommended budget. Final vote June 18. Staffing allocations go to campuses May 8-11.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ⚡ The recommended budget presentation is TODAY (May 21). KVUE's 'There are no good options' headline and the Austin Chronicle's report on 200+ position cuts underscore the severity. Larger class sizes and cuts to teacher planning time at all but the most struggling schools are confirmed. Only Band 1 campuses are protected. This is the point where the budget crisis becomes real for families. The AISD crisis remains directly connected to the housing fight. Declining enrollment (3,000+ students lost) reflects families leaving urban Austin — larger class sizes accelerate the flight. Supporting school quality IS pro-housing policy. Recommended budget TODAY (May 21), final board vote June 18 (28 DAYS). The AISD budget crisis is the backdrop for every November council race conversation. 215 educator cuts and larger class sizes will galvanize parents — a constituency that overlaps with both pro-housing and anti-development camps. The structural problem (state funding formula frozen since 2019) requires state-level advocacy, but the local symptom (budget cuts, school quality) becomes a campaign issue. Support increased housing production as a structural budget solution (more property tax revenue) while advocating for state funding reform. Board vote June 18.

issues.title: Austin Backs Down on ICE Policy — APD Updating General Orders to Cooperate
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): Austin is backing down. On April 24, APD announced it will UPDATE ITS GENERAL ORDERS regarding cooperation with ICE and immigration operations, following Governor Abbott's threat to pull $2.5 million in state public safety grants. The Governor's Public Safety Office granted Austin an extension after what the city described as 'good faith discussions.' Abbott expects any revised policy to require 'full cooperation with DHS, including notifying DHS and detaining individuals subject to removal proceedings.'

This follows the same pattern as Houston (changed its ordinance April 22) and Dallas (revised its ICE policy April 23-24). All three major Texas cities have now yielded to the state's fiscal pressure.

BACKGROUND: The dispute centered on Austin City Council's March 4 revisions to policies governing how APD officers work with Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Under the revised policy, officers were NOT authorized to make arrests or detentions based solely on ICE administrative warrants. The Governor's Public Safety Office demanded repeal or face termination of all public safety grants.

The $2.5M at stake funds public safety programs. In a city facing a $26.4M FY2027 deficit with $16.8M in proposed social services cuts, the fiscal pressure proved decisive. Unlike the 2017 'sanctuary city' fight (when Austin absorbed the consequences), the 2026 fiscal environment left no room to hold the line.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor council response to the deadline. The next council meeting will likely address this issue.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Austin joined Houston and Dallas in yielding to Abbott's fiscal pressure. The $2.5M grant preservation is pragmatically defensible — with 340 APD vacancies and a $26.4M deficit, losing additional public safety funding was untenable. But the pattern matters: all three major Texas cities backed down within a week, demonstrating that state-level fiscal leverage can override local policy preferences. This is relevant context as five council seats turn over in November — candidates' positions on state-vs-local authority and immigrant community trust will matter. The policy reversal may also affect AISD enrollment trends, since immigration enforcement fears were identified as a driver of the 3,000+ student decline.

issues.title: TRUST Act Passed — Austin Regulates Surveillance Technology
issues.summary:
NEW (April 24, 2026): Austin City Council passed the Transparent and Responsible Use of Surveillance Technology Act (TRUST Act), establishing a new regulatory framework for how city departments use surveillance technology.

Key provisions:
• City departments must obtain council approval before deploying new technology that collects data on residents
• Departments must disclose how data is collected, stored, and shared
• Facial recognition technology is BANNED
• Data collection for marketing purposes is prohibited
• Publicly available policies required describing each use of surveillance technology

The ordinance follows a February 2026 council resolution directing the city manager to draft the regulation, which itself followed the June 2025 decision to end Austin's contract with Flock Safety (automatic license plate readers) after community privacy concerns. Council members added amendments during the April 24 vote.

This positions Austin among the most restrictive US cities on government surveillance technology. The TRUST Act reflects growing tension between technology deployment for public safety and civil liberties protections.

public_comment.instructions: Ordinance passed. Monitor implementation and any department technology approval requests that come to council.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The TRUST Act is a significant win for privacy and government transparency — and it connects to the Waymo conversation happening April 29. Austin is establishing a framework for how the city interacts with emerging technology, from surveillance cameras to autonomous vehicles. The facial recognition ban and data disclosure requirements set a high bar that will shape future technology debates. For urbanists, this matters because technology deployment in public spaces affects the livability of dense neighborhoods. Worth monitoring how the ordinance interacts with APD's staffing challenges — if departments can't use certain surveillance tools, it strengthens the case for adequate human staffing.

issues.title: Texas House Passes Historic $100M Childcare Investment (HB 500)
issues.summary:
NEW (May 2026): The Texas House approved HB 500, a supplemental budget bill containing $100 MILLION for child care scholarships. The funding allows the Texas Workforce Commission to serve additional families in its Child Care Services program — critical given that 95,000 families are currently on the waiting list.

This builds on Governor Abbott's earlier signing of HB 117 and HB 4903 — HB 117 streamlines childcare regulations, while HB 4903 creates the Quad-Agency Childcare Initiative Commission to coordinate child care regulation across multiple state agencies.

LOCAL CONTEXT: Austin/Travis County's 'Raising Travis County' voter-approved initiative is in full rollout in 2026 — the fastest implementation of any childcare initiative of its kind in the country and the first in Texas. The $5M county transfer (March 2026) funds an interlocal agreement with the City of Austin for childcare quality improvement. Combined with the state's $100M investment, this creates a rare alignment of city, county, and state childcare funding.

The failure to provide adequate child care costs Texas $11.4 billion annually from productivity and revenue losses.

public_comment.instructions: State legislation. Monitor Texas Workforce Commission for implementation timeline and how Austin/Travis County families can access the new scholarships.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: A rare bipartisan win — $100M for childcare scholarships addresses a critical gap for Austin families. The 95,000-family waitlist is a concrete measure of the crisis. Combined with Raising Travis County's local investment, Austin is now at the intersection of city, county, AND state childcare funding. This creates an opportunity for the Abundance Project agenda: mapping where the new scholarship dollars flow, identifying remaining childcare deserts, and advocating for employer-side solutions to complement public investment.

issues.title: Economic Development Playbook — Council Resolution
issues.summary: On May 7, Council passed a Mayor Watson resolution directing creation of an 'active, progressive' economic development playbook. The framework will set minimum standards and compliance mandates for community benefits attached to future development agreements, recruit new investment, attract international projects, and support local businesses. Watson stated: 'This framework makes Austin's values binding, visible and consistently applied so that every economic decision reflects who we are.' The initiative was supported by Austin's business community and chambers of commerce, and is partly driven by the need to address the city's strained finances by expanding its revenue base amid increased suburban competition for corporate relocations.
issues.why_it_matters_to_you: A formalized economic development playbook could shape which businesses and industries Austin recruits, with community benefit requirements that align with progressive values around equity and affordability. The framework's emphasis on binding community standards could ensure future development deals contribute to affordable housing and equitable growth.

issues.title: Light Rail Transit Overlay Adopted — Transit-Supportive Zoning Along Phase 1 Corridor
issues.summary: NEW (May 16, 2026): Austin City Council adopted the Light Rail Transit Overlay (Ordinance No. 20260516-005) to increase transit-supportive land uses and encourage affordable housing near the Phase 1 Austin Light Rail corridor and its Priority Extensions. The overlay creates new zoning incentives for dense, mixed-use development within walking distance of the 15 planned light rail stations stretching from 38th Street to Oltorf and east from Downtown to East Riverside. This is a critical companion policy to the Project Connect infrastructure investment — ensuring that the $8.23 billion transit system catalyzes housing production and walkable neighborhoods rather than remaining surrounded by parking lots and single-family-only zoning. The overlay responds to national evidence that transit investments without supportive land use policy fail to generate ridership or housing affordability benefits.

public_comment.instructions: Overlay adopted. Monitor implementation and specific station-area plans as they develop.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is the land use policy that makes Project Connect's transit investment pay off. Without transit-supportive zoning, light rail stations get built next to parking lots. The overlay ensures density, mixed-use, and affordable housing incentives around all 15 stations — directly in your 78702 backyard along the East Riverside corridor. Combined with the Density Bonus Program (approved May 22), this creates a comprehensive framework for transit-oriented development. This is YIMBY policy at its best: using public infrastructure investment to unlock private housing production.

issues.title: 12 Shootings Across Austin May 17 — Public Safety Strain Exposed
issues.summary: NEW (May 17, 2026): At least 12 shooting incidents occurred across Austin on May 17, leaving four people wounded. Two teenage suspects and a third suspect were taken into custody. The incidents spanned multiple neighborhoods and overwhelmed police response capacity. APD released a detailed timeline on May 18, identifying three suspects: Cristian Fajardo Mondragon (17), and two juveniles aged 16 and 15. The incidents were a mix of 'random and targeted shootings.' Investigators connected the cases through surveillance footage, ballistic evidence, stolen vehicle reporting, and coordinated intelligence sharing. APD confirmed there is no longer an ongoing threat to the public. This follows the March 1 mass shooting on Sixth Street that killed three and injured more than a dozen, and which the FBI investigated as potential terrorism. The spate of shootings underscores the ongoing strain on APD, which has 340 vacancies out of 1,816 authorized positions (19% vacancy rate). Despite recent progress — APD met its Priority 0/1 response time targets for the first time since August 2023 — the May 17 incidents demonstrate that recovery is fragile. The 157th cadet class (65 cadets) began April 21, but full staffing isn't expected until end of 2027.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor APD and council response. FY2027 budget hearings (July) will address public safety staffing and funding.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Twelve shootings in a single day is a visceral reminder that Austin's public safety recovery is incomplete. The 340 APD vacancies and the March 1 mass shooting already framed this issue — May 17 adds urgency. This will likely dominate the 2026 council elections, with candidates in all five open seats forced to articulate positions on policing, gun violence prevention, and the hospital-based violence prevention programs. The budget context makes it harder: with a $26.4M FY2027 deficit, expanding public safety capacity competes directly with housing, homelessness, and infrastructure investments.

issues.title: 6 Affordable Housing Proposals Receive Council Support for LIHTC Funding
issues.summary: NEW (May 2026): Austin City Council provided resolutions of support for six affordable housing proposals seeking competitive Low Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) funding from the Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs. LIHTC is the primary federal tool for financing affordable housing construction. Council support resolutions are required for competitive applications and signal local government backing. This comes alongside the $46.1 million approved to build and preserve affordable housing in Austin, spanning both new construction and rehabilitation of existing units. The LIHTC pipeline is critical infrastructure for the city's affordable housing goals — these six projects would add hundreds of income-restricted units across Austin.

public_comment.instructions: Resolutions of support passed. Monitor TDHCA for LIHTC award decisions (typically fall 2026).

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: LIHTC is the workhorse of affordable housing finance — these six council-backed projects represent the pipeline of income-restricted housing that complements the market-rate supply expansion driven by HOME Initiative reforms. The $46.1M in approved funding plus the LIHTC pipeline demonstrates Austin's multi-pronged approach to affordability: build more market-rate housing (HOME) while simultaneously investing in income-restricted units (LIHTC, AHFC). Track TDHCA award decisions this fall to see which projects move forward.

issues.title: Citywide Density Bonus Program Approved — New Height/Affordability Framework
issues.summary:
NEW (May 22, 2026): Austin City Council approved sweeping new development rules in the citywide Density Bonus Program. The program allows developers to build 15, 30, 45, or 60 feet taller than current zoning permits in exchange for community benefits — primarily affordable housing units, but also wider sidewalks, trees, or payments into the Housing Trust Fund. The vote passed with only CM Duchen dissenting.

This replaces the controversial DB90 program, which gave a flat 90-foot height bonus and was criticized as too blunt. The new tiered approach calibrates height bonuses to neighborhood context: 15-foot intervals that better match the scale and needs of different areas. Combined with the Light Rail Transit Overlay (adopted May 16) and the HOME Initiative, Austin now has a comprehensive transit-oriented development framework.

CM Ryan Alter noted the program offers 'a range of options' with 'much better outcomes for everybody.' Critics warn it could bring large apartment buildings to neighborhoods where residents oppose them. Housing advocates are cautiously optimistic but want to see if the program actually generates development activity.

**May 29 update:** Downtown DBC (DDB400/DDB850) public hearing held May 28 — extends the density bonus framework into downtown with buildings up to 1,200 feet. Texas SB 840 removed FAR limits for multifamily/mixed-use. Combined with the citywide DBC (May 22), Light Rail Transit Overlay (May 16), and HOME Initiative, Austin's pro-housing zoning framework is the most comprehensive of any major Texas city.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ The Real Deal published analysis (May 29) asking 'Austin's builders can now build taller. Will they?' — examining whether the new DBC framework will actually spur development activity. The question is whether developers take up the new height bonuses or whether market conditions, construction costs, and permitting timelines dampen uptake. Policy without developer uptake is just paper. Monitor for early DBC applications as the leading indicator of whether the framework produces housing.

**May 31 update:** DBC is now law alongside the Downtown DBC districts (DDB400/DDB850 heard May 28). Austin now allows buildings up to 400 feet or 850 feet above base entitlements downtown — maximum heights up to 750 feet (lower tier) and 1,200 feet (higher tier). Developer skepticism persists: Pearlstone Partners CEO Bill Knauss and Urbanspace CEO Kevin Burns 'don't see many differences from this program and programs in the past.' CM Zohaib Quadri added an amendment to explore expanding the program south of Lady Bird Lake. Early DBC applications remain the key indicator of whether policy translates to housing.

**June 19 update:** Juneteenth — city offices CLOSED. Citywide density bonus program in effect since May 22 approval. New rules allow additional 15-60 feet of height depending on zoning and proximity to residential uses. Council on recess until July 23.

public_comment.instructions: Program approved. Monitor for development applications using the new density bonus framework.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This completes the transit-oriented development trifecta: HOME Initiative (2023-24) + Light Rail Transit Overlay (May 16) + Density Bonus Program (May 22). Austin now has the most comprehensive pro-housing zoning framework of any major Texas city. The DBC is the tool that enables taller buildings with affordable units along transit corridors — exactly the kind of density-for-affordability tradeoff YIMBYs champion. Monitor whether developers actually use the program — policy without uptake is just paper.

issues.title: Dog's Head Annexation — 2,600-Acre East Austin Development Approved
issues.summary:
NEW (May 22, 2026): Austin City Council approved a 45-year development agreement to annex and develop more than 2,600 acres along the Colorado River known as the 'Dog's Head' tracts — roughly the size of downtown Austin. The deal allows for mixed-use development with the possibility of housing, commercial, and industrial use. City documents estimate the project could generate $3.5 billion in new property tax revenue over 30 years. Endeavor Real Estate Group is set to be the lead developer.

The vote was controversial — residents largely testified against the deal, which appeared on the council agenda with minimal advance notice and was not vetted by city commissions. Environmental groups raised concerns about one of the most ecologically sensitive stretches of urban river in Texas, as well as flooding risks. Critics also warned the 45-year lock-in prevents future councils from correcting course.

The tracts are hemmed in by a meandering section of the Colorado River east of US-183 and north of SH-130. The site's geography creates both development opportunity and environmental vulnerability. This is one of the largest single annexation/development actions in Austin's history.

public_comment.instructions: Annexation approved. Monitor for specific development plans, environmental impact studies, and community benefit agreements as the 45-year deal is implemented.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: 2,600 acres is a massive development footprint — larger than the 445-acre golf course rezoning. The $3.5B property tax revenue projection and mixed-use potential align with pro-development values, but the rushed process (no commission review, minimal public notice) and 45-year lock-in raise governance concerns. The environmental sensitivity of the Colorado River corridor adds complexity. This deal will shape East Austin for decades. Monitor implementation for housing density commitments, environmental protections, and community benefit provisions.

issues.title: NEW: Gas Peaker Plant — Council Approves ~$1B Plant, Oversight Guidelines
issues.summary:
NEW (May 2026): Austin City Council voted behind closed doors to approve Austin Energy's plan to build a new 400-megawatt natural gas peaker plant, with costs estimated around $1 billion. The closed-door vote sparked backlash from environmental advocates and government watchdogs who accused city leaders of shielding a controversial public decision from scrutiny. Because the city operates Austin Energy, state law allows City Council to privately vote on 'competitive matters' as the utility's governing body.

On May 29, Council approved oversight guidelines for the plant, directing Austin Energy to: (1) limit use of older gas peaker units at the Decker Creek facility to 'times of extreme scarcity,' (2) cap annual CO2 and nitrogen oxide emissions, and (3) follow new construction and operation standards. Council members said the rules will help reduce emissions and harmful local impacts.

Environmental groups (Solar Austin, Sierra Club) oppose the plant, arguing it contradicts Austin's climate goals and locks in fossil fuel infrastructure for decades. Austin Energy argues gas peakers are an 'unfortunate necessity' to maintain grid reliability as Texas faces growing electricity demand from data centers and population growth. The price tag could top $1 billion. The plant's location has not been formally disclosed.

This issue cuts across energy, environmental, fiscal, and transparency concerns — and will likely feature in November council races.

public_comment.instructions: Guidelines approved. Monitor Austin Energy for siting decisions and construction timeline. Engage through council budget hearings for FY2027.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: A ~$1B gas peaker plant is a major fiscal and environmental commitment that will shape Austin's energy trajectory for decades. The closed-door vote raises governance transparency concerns that align with your support for accountable government. The oversight guidelines (emissions caps, restricted use of older units) are pragmatic guardrails, but the underlying question — whether Austin should be building new fossil fuel infrastructure — will be a litmus test in November council races. Monitor for siting decisions, which will have neighborhood-level impacts.

issues.title: Austin Approves Sweeping Development Rule Changes — Lot Size, Missing Middle, Mixed-Use
issues.summary:
NEW (June 3, 2026): In a landmark week for Austin housing policy, the City Council approved multiple development rule changes in late May:

• **Minimum Lot Size** (May 26): Reduced from 5,750 sq ft to 1,800 sq ft citywide — the first change in over 80 years. Allows smaller, cheaper homes and enables more homes per block.

• **Density Bonus Program** (May 22): Developers can build 15-60 feet taller in exchange for affordable housing units or community benefits (wider sidewalks, green space). Only CM Duchen voted against. Replaces the controversial DB90 program.

• **Missing Middle Zoning** (May 2026): New zoning districts created for smaller housing types — townhomes, cottage courts, small multiplexes — that fill the gap between single-family homes and large apartment buildings.

• **Mixed-Use Zoning** (May 2026): New districts allowing commercial and residential development in tandem, enabling walkable neighborhood commercial corridors.

• **Dog's Head Annexation** (May 22): 2,600-acre annexation approved with a 45-year development agreement. 20% of housing must be affordable. Projected $3.5B in property tax revenue over the agreement term.

Combined with the HOME Initiative and Light Rail Transit Overlay (adopted May 16), Austin now has the most comprehensive pro-housing zoning framework of any major Texas city. Pew Research (March 2026) credited Austin's housing construction surge with driving down rents — these reforms extend that trajectory.

The Real Deal asked (May 29): 'Austin's builders can now build taller. Will they?' The answer depends on market conditions, interest rates, and developer uptake of the new DBC framework.

**June 4 update:** Implementation phase underway. Council on summer recess until July 23. Development community assessing DBC uptake — Real Deal (May 30) questioned whether Austin's builders will actually use new height bonuses.

**June 19 update:** Juneteenth — city offices CLOSED. Development rules overhaul implementation continuing. SB 840 compliance driving changes to floor-area ratio regulations. Council on recess until July 23.

public_comment.instructions: Reforms adopted. Monitor implementation through permitting data. The DBC uptake rate will be the key indicator of whether zoning reform produces actual housing.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is the YIMBY policy trifecta Austin has been building toward: HOME Initiative (housing by right) + Density Bonus (height for affordability) + Light Rail Overlay (transit-oriented density) + lot size reduction (enabling smaller homes). Pew Research credited Austin's construction surge with driving down rents. These reforms lock in the pro-building trajectory for years. The key now is implementation — monitor DBC uptake, track permitting timelines, and support the reforms against any rollback attempts from the next council. The November council elections will determine whether this trajectory continues.
